title: Left lower quadrant pain and fever

chart.oneLiner: 58M presents with 3 days of constant left lower quadrant pain, low-grade fever, and a change in bowel habits.

opening: I've had a steady ache in my lower left belly for a few days, and I've been running a fever.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 58-year-old man, mildly uncomfortable. Cooperative.

HPI: 3 days of constant left lower quadrant pain, low-grade fevers, mild nausea, and altered bowel habits (some constipation then loose stools). Reduced appetite. NO large-volume rectal bleeding, NO vomiting, NO prior similar episodes (mention prior episode only if asked).
PMH: hypertension; low-fiber diet. MEDS: lisinopril. SOCIAL: nonsmoker, social alcohol.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: comfortable at rest, mild distress on exam.
- Vitals: BP 134/82, HR 92, RR 16, 38.0°C, SpO2 99% RA.
- Abdomen: LEFT lower quadrant tenderness with mild voluntary guarding; no diffuse rebound or rigidity (uncomplicated); no palpable mass; bowel sounds present.
- No costovertebral angle tenderness. Cardiac/lungs: normal.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the low-fiber diet or any prior episode unless asked about diet or prior similar pain.
- LLQ findings are revealed only if the abdomen is examined.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.CBC: WBC 13.0 with left shift, Hgb 14.2, Plt 280.
labs.CT_abdomen: CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast: sigmoid colon diverticula with wall thickening and pericolic fat stranding; NO abscess, free air, or fistula — uncomplicated diverticulitis.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Sinus rhythm interrupted by frequent premature atrial complexes with abnormal P morphology and noncompensatory pauses.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: Sinus rhythm interrupted by frequent premature atrial complexes with abnormal P morphology and noncompensatory pauses. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Healed/healing rib fractures with callus along the lateral ribs; lungs clear.
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: Healed/healing rib fractures with callus along the lateral ribs; lungs clear. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
cxr.imageDescription: chest radiograph showing old healed rib fractures

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential diagnosis (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Acute diverticulitis (leading,8). Colorectal cancer (CANT-MISS to consider/exclude later,4). Inflammatory bowel disease / colitis (core,3). Ischemic colitis (core,3). Urinary cause (core,2). In appropriate patients, gynecologic causes (bonus,2). Named leading dx w/ justification — LLQ pain, fever, leukocytosis (core,3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Acute diverticulitis. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Colorectal cancer. Also reasonable: Inflammatory bowel disease / colitis; Ischemic colitis; Urinary cause.

coreActions.item: Named leading dx w/ justification — LLQ pain, fever, leukocytosis

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial diagnostic workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast (CANT-MISS,5). CBC (3). BMP (2). Urinalysis (2). Considers stool studies if diarrhea predominant (bonus,1). PENALTY: colonoscopy DURING acute diverticulitis (perforation risk) (-3); shotgun panel w/ no reasoning (-2).

bonusActions.item: Considers stool studies if diarrhea predominant

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = normal sinus rhythm, no acute changes. CREDIT: rate & rhythm (7); recognizes a normal tracing (5); systematic read (3). PENALTY: over-reading a normal EKG (-2).
keyFindings: distinguishes PACs from PVCs (narrow, abnormal P)
commonMistakes: mistaking PACs for an irregular sustained atrial arrhythmia
steps.idealAnswer: Sinus rhythm with frequent premature atrial complexes (early abnormal P waves, narrow QRS).

coreActions: distinguishes PACs from PVCs (narrow, abnormal P)

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = no free air, clear bases. CREDIT: identifies a normal film (5); excludes free air (perforation) (5). PENALTY: inventing pathology (-2).
keyFindings: identifies healed rib fractures with callus
commonMistakes: mistaking callus for a pulmonary or pleural lesion
steps.idealAnswer: Old healed rib fractures with bony callus along the lateral ribs.

coreActions: identifies healed rib fractures with callus

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Given the CT, what is your diagnosis and severity?
steps.rubric: Uncomplicated acute (sigmoid) diverticulitis (6). Cites diverticula + wall thickening + fat stranding WITHOUT abscess/perforation (3). Notes the plan for interval colonoscopy after resolution to exclude malignancy (bonus,1).

bonusActions.item: Notes the plan for interval colonoscopy after resolution to exclude malignancy

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH abdominal pain / intra-abdominal infection: antibiotics covering GNR + anaerobes – cipro+metronidazole or amox-clav (CANT-MISS,6); bowel rest / diet advancement (4); analgesia (3); CT + drainage + surgery if complicated (abscess/perforation) (5); interval colonoscopy after resolution (3). PENALTY: missing complicated disease needing drainage/surgery (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Uncomplicated diverticulitis: bowel rest/clear liquids, analgesia, and antibiotics covering GNRs + anaerobes (ciprofloxacin + metronidazole, or amoxicillin-clavulanate). Complicated disease (abscess, perforation, obstruction) → CT, IV antibiotics, drainage of abscess, surgical evaluation. Outpatient colonoscopy 6–8 weeks after resolution.
steps.disposition: Admit if complicated/unable to tolerate PO

standardizedPatient.communicationStyle: answers questions asked without volunteering diagnostic clues
disclosureRules: Do not reveal sensitive history unless directly asked
Do not mention or hint at the diagnosis
Do not volunteer hidden exam or diagnostic findings
Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 58M presents with 3 days of constant left lower quadrant pain, low-grade fever, and a change in bowel habits. The diagnosis is Diverticulitis, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.

reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Diverticulitis while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Diverticulitis and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.
criticalInterpretationItems: Normal sinus rhythm; no acute changes.
No free air under the diaphragm; clear lung bases (excludes perforation).
imageInterpretationPearls: An incidental abnormality is an interpretation task in its own right — name it, then judge its relevance.
Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
